AtomicFact.description: A single atomic fact extracted from a source.

direct_quote.description: Exact quote from source supporting this fact
direct_quote.title: Direct Quote

fact_category.description: Category: statistic, measurement, causal_link, named_entity, date_time, regulatory_threshold, standard_reference, geographic, methodology
fact_category.title: Fact Category

relevance_score.description: Relevance to the research question, 0.0 to 1.0
relevance_score.title: Relevance Score

confidence.description: Confidence that this fact is correctly extracted, 0.0 to 1.0
confidence.title: Confidence

perspective.description: STORM research perspective: Scientific, Regulatory, Industry, Economic, Public_Health, Historical, Regional, Methodological, Emerging_Trends
perspective.title: Perspective

entities.description: Key named entities (compounds, methods, organizations, metrics) mentioned in this fact. Used by the wiki mesh entity canonicalization pass. Optional; legacy data defaults to [].
entities.title: Entities

AtomicFact.title: AtomicFact

SourceAnalysis.description: Analysis of a single source document.

source_type.description: Type: journal_article, government_report, industry_report, news, standard, patent, book, other
source_type.title: Source Type

overall_relevance.description: Overall relevance to the research question, 0.0 to 1.0
overall_relevance.title: Overall Relevance

atomic_facts.description: All atomic facts extracted from this source
atomic_facts.title: Atomic Facts

evidence_summary.description: Brief summary of what this source contributes
evidence_summary.title: Evidence Summary

SourceAnalysis.title: SourceAnalysis

system:
You are a research evidence analyst. Extract atomic facts from source material.

Rules:
1. Extract 8-15 atomic facts per source. Focus on the MOST relevant, specific, and unique claims, statistics, findings, and conclusions. Quality over quantity — prioritize facts that DIRECTLY answer the research question over tangential details. Missing a highly relevant fact is worse than including marginally relevant ones.
   CRITICAL: ONLY extract from the ARTICLE BODY TEXT. NEVER extract from: navigation menus, cookie consent banners, URL paths/slugs, abbreviation/glossary tables, author byline metadata, page footers/headers, table of contents page numbers, product category labels, or marketing taglines. If the source is mostly non-content (nav, ads, cookie text), extract ZERO facts and set source_quality to 0.0.
2. Each atomic fact = ONE specific claim with the EXACT quote from the source (minimum 10 words, up to 200 chars). A quote shorter than 5 words is NEVER acceptable — skip that fact entirely.
3. Source quality: Score each source individually on 0.0-1.0 based on:
   - Peer review status (peer-reviewed journal > preprint > report > blog)
   - Author credentials (named experts > organizations > anonymous)
   - Recency (2023-2026 > 2018-2022 > older)
   - Specificity to research question (directly relevant > tangentially related)
   Do NOT use fixed scores for source types. Vary scores based on each source's specific qualities.
4. Rate relevance to the SPECIFIC research question on 0.0-1.0:
   - 0.9-1.0: Directly answers part of the research question with specific data
   - 0.7-0.8: Provides important context or supporting evidence
   - 0.5-0.6: Tangentially relevant or provides background
   - 0.1-0.4: Marginally relevant, mostly off-topic
   Each fact must get a UNIQUE relevance score. No two facts from the same source should have identical scores.
5. Include exact numbers, dates, measurements, names, standards.
6. For EVERY quantitative finding, extract ALL of the following when available:
   - Effect size with units (MD, SMD, WMD, OR, HR, RR)
   - 95% confidence interval (e.g., "95% CI -5.54 to -3.05")
   - Heterogeneity (I-squared value, e.g., "I²=96%")
   - Number of studies/trials included (e.g., "7 RCTs")
   - Total sample size (e.g., "n=269")
   - Evidence certainty/quality rating (e.g., "GRADE: moderate", "high certainty")
   - Study duration/follow-up period
   Missing any of these when the source provides them is a CRITICAL extraction failure.
7. For review/meta-analysis papers, extract findings from EACH cited study individually.
   Prioritize pooled estimates with I² and CI over narrative summaries.
8. For each atomic fact, tag the research perspective it belongs to.
9. CRITICAL (ARCH-4): The 'statement' field must be a DIRECT PARAPHRASE of the source text.
   Do NOT infer, extrapolate, or add information beyond what the direct_quote states.
   If the direct_quote is fewer than 10 words, the statement must be equally narrow in scope.
10. QUOTE LENGTH: The 'direct_quote' field MUST be 150 characters or fewer.
   Quote only the KEY phrase, not entire sentences or paragraphs.
   This forces you to identify the most important fragment of evidence.
   BAD: "A systematic review and meta-analysis of 24 randomized controlled trials totaling 1,768 participants found that alternate-day fasting produced weight loss comparable to continuous energy restriction"
   GOOD: "alternate-day fasting produced weight loss comparable to continuous energy restriction"
   A 2-word quote like 'distilled water' can only support a statement about distilled water,
   not a full sentence about healthcare settings or bacterial contamination.
   Perspectives: Scientific, Regulatory, Industry, Economic, Public_Health, Historical, Regional, Methodological, Emerging_Trends.
   Choose the single best-fit perspective.

Output format (return ONLY this JSON structure):
{"analyses": [{"source_url": "https://example.com", "source_title": "Study Title", "source_type": "journal_article", "source_quality": 0.8, "overall_relevance": 0.7, "year": 2024, "authors": ["Smith J"], "venue": "Water Research", "doi": "", "atomic_facts": [{"statement": "E. coli was detected in 30% of tested filters after 6 months", "direct_quote": "E. coli presence in 30% of tested POU filters", "fact_category": "statistic", "relevance_score": 0.9, "confidence": 0.8, "perspective": "Scientific"}], "evidence_summary": "Bacterial contamination in POU water filters"}]}
prompt:
Research question: What are the current best-available estimates of permafrost carbon release under RCP 4.5 and RCP 8.5 scenarios by 2100?

Analyze the following 2 sources and extract the TOP 8-15 MOST
relevant atomic facts per source. Focus on the most specific, unique, and
directly relevant numbers, dates, measurements, claims, findings,
regulations, and standards. Quality over quantity.

Sources:
Source URL: https://journals.ametsoc.org/view/journals/wcas/17/3/WCAS-D-24-0150.1.xml
Source title: Environmental Impacts from Projected Permafrost Thaw in Alaska: Defining Knowledge Gaps, Data Needs, and Research Priorities
Source type: academic
Content:
Abstract Thawing permafrost will significantly impact regulatory activities in Alaska that are conducted by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) to protect human health and the environment. Many of those activities are in vulnerable rural lands predominantly occupied by Alaska Natives. Temperatures in Alaska—the largest and northernmost of the 50 states—are increasing disproportionately relative to the global average and at a markedly higher rate than in any other state in the Nation. Most land and surface waters in Alaska are underlaid by permafrost (or perennially frozen ground), which stabilizes infrastructure, serves community needs, and shapes ecosystems with unique environmental characteristics. The Fourth National Climate Assessment estimated that 16%–24% of near-surface Alaskan permafrost could disappear by the end of this century. Permafrost thaw not only compromises critical infrastructure used in daily life (e.g., buildings, roads, and pipelines) but also alters the movement of hazardous substances within the surrounding environment. Changes to permafrost characteristics and extent may release contaminants through multiple mechanisms, which can pollute air, soil, and water, with unintended consequences to subsistence activities and the livelihood of local communities. This paper summarizes information gathered from semiformal interviews conducted with EPA regulatory staff during late 2022 to glean the breadth of activities that would be affected by permafrost thaw, tabulate available data sources, and identify data and knowledge gaps. This information can be used to influence monitoring activities and future research that can inform EPA regulatory activities affected by permafrost thaw in Alaska. Significance Statement Permafrost (or perennially frozen ground) is widespread in Alaska. It serves vital roles for communities and ecosystems, including infrastructure support, natural refrigeration, and insulation from contaminant seepage from waste disposal and other sources. Rapid warming in Alaska is compromising permafrost, which can create adverse environmental and health risks. Internal interviews at the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency revealed knowledge and data gaps to improve the understanding of the current state and future characteristics of permafrost. Regulators need to understand the extent and depth of permafrost—and the timing and changes to that permafrost—at a fine spatial resolution to inform adaptation activities in partnership with state, local, and Tribal leaders. Filling those gaps facilitates protecting the people, resources, and ecosystems in Alaska. © 2025 American Meteorological Society. This published article is licensed under the terms of the default AMS reuse license. For information regarding reuse of this content and general copyright information, consult the AMS Copyright Policy (www.ametsoc.org/PUBSReuseLicenses).
---Source URL: https://cdnsciencepub.com/doi/abs/10.1139/er-2024-0001
Source title: 10-year progress on forest carbon research in Canada
Source type: academic
Content:
Global changes in temperature and precipitation, and other factors such as increases in the atmospheric concentration of CO2 and the deposition of nitrogen, can influence forest carbon through changes in forest growth, decay, and disturbance. Climate variability impacts both forest growth and decomposition of DOM, and so increased growth does not always translate to an increase in ecosystem carbon stocks. Although environmental change impacts on biophysical processes of forest ecosystems have not been quantified for Canada, progress has been made on better understanding impacts on forest growth and decomposition, peatlands and lateral fluxes, and changes in disturbance regimes.
3.2.1. Forest growth, mortality, and recruitment
Understanding the impacts of climate requires the reconstruction of forest growth over long temporal scales to examine its relationship to past climate and other factors, and the magnitude and causes of inter-annual variation and trends. Climate responses are regionally differentiated and may be both positive and negative, varying as a function of species, region, tree size, and competition status.
Marchand et al. (2018) reviewed 44 Canadian studies addressing the question of forest growth trends at the site, regional, and national scales, and found an equal proportion of studies with positive and negative trends, but also that the conclusions depended on the type of data used (tree-rings, forest plots, or remote sensing) and the analytical method. Continued efforts to resolve the reasons for these differences is of fundamental scientific and policy importance because projections of future forest growth underlie all of Canada's commitments to sustainable forest management”. Climate-sensitive growth models are needed to predict future ecosystem carbon removals, but a recent review (
Metsaranta et.al. 2024) found that many of the growth models that support sustainable forest management planning currently lack climate sensitivity, and none include annual climate sensitivity. Some individual components of some Canadian growth and yield models have developed climate-sensitive aspects, for example, the diameter and height growth functions of the mixedwood growth model (
Oboite and Comeau 2021) and the climate-sensitive site index models for plantations in Ontario (
Sharma and Parton 2019).
Various approaches that consider biometric, tree-ring, remotely sensed and eddy covariance datasets to support environmentally sensitive growth, mortality, and recruitment models, and their associated carbon fluxes. Biometric data include periodic increment, mortality, and recruitment from re-measuring sample plots. Nationally, re-measurements are available for Canada's National Forest Inventory (NFI) plots that measure all forest carbon pools, including soil and DOM
5. Provincial forest management agencies maintain similar plot databases, mainly for aboveground biomass carbon, and these are being coordinated, standardized, and shared (multi-agency ground plot, MAGPlot). In addition, academic research networks like ForestGEO
6 (a consortium led by the Smithsonian Institute) are assembling compilations of ecosystem data, including carbon flux data. The Smartforests Canada initiative
7 is a similar network for Canada. However, not all possible sites in Canada participate in these networks. At present, these data networks provide good ground-based data coverage for periodic tree growth and mortality, which contribute to an understanding of the net aboveground increment portion of NPP. However, aboveground increment represents a minor fraction (10%–15%) of NPP (
Smyth et al. 2013;
Pappas et al. 2020), and there are currently no efforts in place to develop large-scale observational datasets of the other components that make up the largest portion of NPP and that require more intensive measurement, namely, aboveground litterfall, root biomass, and root turnover. This represents a significant knowledge gap.
Tree-ring approaches measure annual growth rings of individual trees, and intensive sampling of all live and dead trees, or appropriate statistical sampling schemes, allow tree-level annual growth to be scaled to stand-level annual growth and mortality. A large Canada-wide database of tree-ring measurements was recently compiled to support estimates of tree and forest growth trends as well as carbon fluxes (
Girardin et al. 2021). Some hybrid biometric-model flux estimates show good agreement with flux estimates derived from measurements at eddy covariance installations (
Metsaranta et al. 2018,
2021). This might open the possibility of wide-ranging scaling of tree-level growth increments to stand level ecosystem fluxes. However, the two approaches may not agree in inter-annual variation terms when there is a time lag between photosynthesis and allocation to wood production at breast height (
Teets et al. 2018;
Lagergren et al. 2019;
Metsaranta et al. 2021;
Cabon et al. 2022). Tree-ring studies continue to address the causes of inter-annual variation in forest growth, the importance of tree size and competition factors, relative to climate, and projections under different climate scenarios. Many of these studies are regional in nature, focusing on western Canada (
Jiang et al. 2018) and Quebec (
D'Orangeville et al. 2018;
Marchand et al. 2019). There is currently no meta-analytic study of the tree-ring-based causes of inter-annual variation in growth or the relative importance of climate, tree size, and competition factors as drivers for all studies across Canada. However, there are national-scale studies based on NFI data (
Girardin et al. 2016) and the CFS-TRenD data (
Girardin et al. 2021) that have studied the magnitude and causes of inter-annual variation and trends at the national scale. Possible methodological shortcomings have been recognized for sample plot data (
Searle and Chen 2017) and for tree-ring data (
Hember et al. 2019). For example, minimum size limits for measurement in sample plot networks will underestimate growth in young stands when most trees are smaller than this limit. For tree-rings, sampling large trees and climate sensitive sites may overestimate climate effects. Resolving these shortcomings will be required to detect growth trends more reliably. Tree-ring data continue to be a key data source for understanding inter-annual variation and trends in forest growth, particularly when large-scale data compilations are developed as recent studies have suggested (
Evans et al. 2022).
Eddy covariance installations measure exchanges of CO
2, water, and energy between the atmosphere and the forest and provide high temporal resolution estimates of net fluxes for relatively small areas (i.e., the tower footprint), and these data are used in national and global scale analyses of various carbon fluxes (
Campioli et al. 2016). From 1994 to 2014, FLUXNET Canada, comprised of the Fluxnet-Canada Research Network and the Canadian Carbon Program, operated a coordinated network of eddy covariance sites across Canada. There are now few eddy covariance sites, and they operate with less coordination after FLUXNET Canada ended in 2014
8, but data from these sites allow researchers to refine the scientific understanding of ecosystem carbon fluxes at the site level and to improve modelling parameters used in process-based models.
Repeated point cloud data acquisitions allowed to characterize forest growth accurately (
Tompalski et al. 2019) and link to existing growth simulators (
Lamb et al. 2018;
Tompalski et al. 2018), enabling fine-scale projections of forest attributes. Airborne and satellite laser scanning combined with national inventory photo plot attributes were used to generate Canada-wide forest attribute maps (
Margolis et al. 2015). By linking inventory plots with the Canada-wide MODIS forest change product, a time series of aboveground biomass estimates was developed at 250 m pixel size (
Beaudoin et al. 2018). Similarly, annual maps of aboveground biomass and other forest attributes were also developed at 30 m resolution starting in 1984 (
Matasci et al. 2018b). Additional estimates of forest biomass stocks from remotely sensed data (
Bolton et al. 2018), ground plots (
Lopes Queiroz et al. 2020), and time since disturbance (
Vijayakumar et al. 2017) will be useful for future comparisons.
Carbon dynamics have been modelled using process- and inventory-based models at regional, landscape, and national scales, and there remains a split between models used for forest management (mainly empirical or inventory models, including growth and yield models) and models used for research (usually process or hybrid models). Typically, the carbon consequences of disturbances, particularly anthropogenic disturbances, are well represented in inventory models, and the effects of climatic and global change forcings are well represented in process models (
Kurz et al. 2013). Process models incorporate future climate and ecosystem properties to determine tree recruitment, growth rates, succession, disturbances, and the ecosystem carbon stocks (
Newton 2016;
Trugman et al. 2016). Global coupled climate models or land surface models include disturbances and climate change and simulate large landscapes using simplified forests at high temporal resolution but low spatial resolution (
Arora et al. 2016;
Melton et al. 2020). Comparisons of net ecosystem exchange using top-down (terrestrial biosphere models) and bottom-up (inventory-based) approaches or process model estimates are useful for refining and improving models and lead to more accurate representations of ecosystem processes and dynamics (
Hayes et al. 2012;
Huntzinger et al. 2012). Detailed model intercomparison studies at fluxnet sites in the early 2010s often showed disagreement in the direction and magnitude of fluxes (
Wang et al. 2011,
2013). While a more recent multimodel study shows more agreement in climate change impact terms (
Boulanger et al. 2022), there are still discrepancies between top-down (inversion) and bottom-up (inventory-based) estimates of ecosystem fluxes for Canada and other countries (
Deng et al. 2022).
3.2.2. Treed and forested peatlands, and lateral fluxes
Forested landscapes are a mosaic of upland forests, lowland forests, and wetlands, including peatlands such as open, treed, and forested bogs, and fens. Peatlands are an important component of carbon budget; at a national scale, peatlands emit 3.9–6.9 Tg CH
4-C year
−1 and, by various estimates, can emit 6.6 Tg CO₂-C year
−1 or uptake 37.8 Tg CO₂-C year
−1 (
Webster et al. 2018;
Bona et al. 2024). Over the last decade, efforts have improved national-scale peatland mapping (
Thompson et al. 2016), developed peat properties databases (e.g., peat depth, and carbon and nutrient concentrations), derived peatland-specific biomass functions, and collated methane and net ecosystem exchange observations from peatlands across Canada for model development (
Webster et al. 2018). Those efforts led to the creation of a moss model (MOSS-C (
Bona et al. 2016)), which is used in black spruce-dominated forest stands, and the Canadian Model for Peatlands (CaMP). The CaMP was developed as a module of the generic carbon budget model (GCBM) to estimate growth, turnover, and decay within 11 peatland categories (
Bona et al. 2020). It was parameterized using the values reported in the literature (
Bona et al. 2018) as well as via calibration against a peat profile carbon stock database (
Bona et al. 2020). With these modelling improvements, national-scale assessment systems such as the NFCMARS are now poised to more realistically model the carbon dynamics of both moss-dominated upland black spruce stands and peatlands, a significant improvement achieved during the last decade.
The importance of export to aquatic systems in carbon budgets is now increasingly recognized. The exported carbon can be metabolized and emitted into the atmosphere as CO
2 and methane or buried in wetland or lake sediments, where it could stay for millennia due to slow decay and low susceptibility to disturbances. In Western Canada, dissolved organic carbon exports increased in the McKenzie River basin between 1970s and 2010s, and the increases were attributed mainly to the thawing permafrost (
Tank et al. 2016). Modelling of carbon fluxes in subarctic and thawing permafrost landscapes composed of mixed wetlands and forests identified wetlands as the dominant methane emission source and highlighted the potential for rapid expansion of permafrost-free wetlands due to warming, creating a positive climate–carbon cycle feedback (
Lundin et al. 2016;
Helbig et al. 2017). Even though wetlands emitted a lot of methane, a limited amount of thawing permafrost carbon was converted to methane within wetlands (
Cooper et al. 2017). Warming-induced thermokarst collapse was shown to cause mineralization of previously frozen carbon; however, new carbon accumulation rates offset the frozen carbon loss within 200 years (
Estop-Aragonés et al. 2018;
Heffernan et al. 2020). To understand GHG release from permafrost thaw, a framework has been developed that considers methane and CO
2 from soil decay based on carbon availability and quality, as well as soil temperature and wetness (
Webster and McLaughlin 2014). The interface between forests and freshwater and marine ecosystems contributes significantly to global carbon cycles and is inherently linked to forest carbon (
Cole et al. 2007;
Cavallaro et al. 2018). Over the next decade, the ability to model these exports in large-scale assessments should see significant improvements.
3.2.3. Soil and dead organic matter dynamics
DOM stored in litter, coarse woody debris, and soils forms the largest terrestrial pool of carbon, and is driven by the balance between fresh carbon input and decomposition. Over the past 10 years, studies have advanced our understanding of the processes that drive biomass turnover and decomposition. Measurements of soil organic matter and woody debris were incorporated into the NFI, and a new comprehensive Canadian upland forest soil profile and carbon stocks database was compiled (
Shaw et al. 2018). In addition, mapping of soil carbon and other soil properties at local to regional to national scales was improved (
Hugelius et al. 2013;
Sothe et al. 2022).
Several studies published in the past 10 years provided insights into controls of carbon decay rates. In western Canada, carbon decay in litter, organic, and surface mineral layers was likely strongly affected by moisture conditions as indicated by the strong moisture control on potential microbial enzyme activity (
Brockett et al. 2012). Tree species was found to affect the decay rate of the organic layer, with faster rates in trembling aspen-dominated stands compared to conifer-dominated stands (
Laganière et al. 2013). Litterfall chemistry, particularly the proportion of mosses in the litterfall, was also identified as the driver of soil chemistry and decomposability across the latitudinal transect in boreal Canada, highlighting that mosses provide slow-decomposing inputs that diminish with lower latitudes or warmer climates (
Kohl et al. 2018). Temperature was also a driving factor for soil decay, with higher decay rates for warmer temperatures, and species impacts on temperature sensitivity of soil decay in eastern boreal Canada (
Laganière et al. 2012). Understanding future warming impacts on decay is uncertain; a study in Atlantic Canada revealed that soil organic carbon is unlikely to change with warming due to increased carbon inputs to the soil compensating for elevated decay (
Ziegler et al. 2017), and a recent incubation experiment of thawed permafrost soils found that the rate of loss of carbon is slow (
Dieleman et al. 2022).
3.2.4. Climate change impacts
Predictions of future impacts could involve rapid ecosystem shifts, which will increase carbon emissions to the atmosphere. In a review of climate change impacts on Canada's boreal forests, potential tipping points were identified as warming climates cause permafrost thaw and change disturbances, allow survival of overwintering beetles leading to outbreaks, and cause drought-induced dieback of aspen to shrublands or grasslands (
Price et al. 2013).
Studies continued to suggest that fire weather conditions will become more severe in the future (
Oris et al. 2014;
Kirchmeier‐Young et al. 2019), which will translate into an increase in future area burned across the landscape, but with high uncertainty (
Boulanger et al. 2018). It has been generally understood for many years that more severe wildfire disturbance regimes will likely cause losses of carbon from Canadian forests, and that increases in forest productivity would not offset losses from increased disturbances. This is broadly supported, e.g., (
Miquelajauregui et al. 2019;
Wang et al. 2021;
Zhao et al. 2021), with exceptions in some regions now suggesting that increases in forest production (
Gonsamo et al. 2017) or a shift from coniferous to deciduous trees, whether actively (
Girardin and Terrier 2015) or post-fire (
Mack et al. 2021), could offset losses from increased wildfire disturbance. Of note is the finding that the soil carbon that was not burned during previous wildfire in boreal forests (“legacy carbon”) is going to be lost to the next wildfire if the return interval decreases to 60 years or less (
Walker et al. 2019), decreasing the likelihood of recovery of slow-decomposing carbon stocks post fire. Wildfires may also cause conversion of forests to non-forest or broadleaf species (
Girard et al. 2008;
Splawinski et al. 2019;
Coop et al. 2020;
Baltzer et al. 2021), with burned areas and fire severity increasing and forest recovery compromised by a lack of seed sources and warmer and drier post-fire climate. In addition, previously resilient landscapes, such as permafrost-dominated landscapes may be compromised by warming and experience post-fire transitions that result in rapid and irreversible permafrost degradation (
Holloway et al. 2020).
The 2023 wildfire season in Canada was unprecedented, with the largest area burned in the historical record (up to 17 Mha burned
9), and records for annual burned areas set in four provinces and one territory. The prediction of events larger than those in a historical record is a branch of statistics known as extreme value analysis, and a common statistic derived from extreme values analysis is the size of the largest event expected to occur once every 100 years (
Milly et al. 2008). Recent research has demonstrated the importance of these large, rare events to forest sustainability (
Nelson and Scorah 2021) and GHG emissions and removals (
Metsaranta et al. 2023). In a review of Canadian wildfire risk research,
Johnston et al. (2020) acknowledged the importance of extreme fire weather conditions to future risk, but did not suggest any formal methods for quantifying probabilities of extreme areas burned in the future. The log-normal characterization of area burned (
Metsaranta 2010) facilitates inference about future large events, and research over the next decade could enhance focus on the prediction of extreme fire year probabilities, rather than using central tendency statistics such as fire return intervals or fire cycles that traditionally characterize wildfire regimes.
Previous studies of the potential impact of future insect outbreaks on forest carbon (e.g.,
Kurz et al. 2008;
Dymond et al. 2010) forecasted future areas and impacts under a simple set of assumptions that depended on probabilistic characterizations of outbreak intervals, outbreak durations, and areas affected (e.g., Ch. 8,
Barreiro et al. 2017;
Stephens et al. 2018). Comprehensive reviews of the potential impact of climate change on forest insects (
Pureswaran et al. 2015) and biotic disturbances in general, inclusive of forest diseases and invasive species (
Weed et al. 2013;
Ramsfield et al. 2016), were all conducted in the last decade. Overall, methods for predicting future pest outbreaks under climate change require further development, although some approaches are available and decision support systems for short-term forecasting of major forest pest insects (mountain pine beetle and spruce budworm) continue to be supported (
MacLean 2016). Insufficient data on past and present outbreaks have been identified as a limitation for forecasting future outbreaks (
Gray 2017).
Warming may also increase the frequency of extreme weather and climate events (e.g.,
Walsh et al. (2020)). Forest vulnerability to windthrow has been assessed from storms in Ontario (e.g., using ForestGALES (
Anyomi et al. 2017)), or to ice storms in eastern North America (
Klima and Granger Morgan 2015) or to drought-induced mortality in many parts of Canada (
Aubin et al. 2018). A national map of forest vulnerability found that boreal forests in central Alberta and Saskatchewan were vulnerable to drought in the mid- to long-term and that regions in the Rockies were vulnerable to migration failure (
Aubin et al. 2018).
Warming can shift species distribution and cause permafrost degradation, which, in turn, leads to a change in forest community composition or forest loss. Impacts of climate change on tree species suitability in British Columbia project rapid shifts in suitability to higher elevations and latitudes relative to their current range (
Flower et al. 2013), and selection models have been developed that consider species-specific projections of reforestation feasibility with climate change (
MacKenzie and Mahony 2021). Within the Northwest Territories, trends in tree demography that are driven by permafrost thaw suggest that spruce-dominated plateaus are likely to be converted to larch-dominated wetlands as the thaw progresses (
Dearborn et al. 2021). It is estimated that around 9% of forests were converted into peatlands as a result of permafrost thaw between 1977 and 2010 in the Scotty Creek watershed in Northwest Territories (
Baltzer et al. 2014). Similarly, 6.9%–11.6% of forests in northern British Columbia and south Northwest Territories were lost as a result of permafrost thaw between 1970 and 2010 (
Carpino et al. 2018).
Future anthropogenic disturbance regime forecasts, as characterized by area or volume of wood harvested, continue to be conducted by provincial forest management agencies through legally mandated timber or wood supply assessment processes. Uncertainty about future natural disturbances critically influences these analyses (
Melo et al. 2019), and research has shown that harvest levels in some areas may not be sustainable under increases in future area burned (
Gauthier et al. 2015). In an expansive nation such as Canada, the feasibility of transporting harvested wood over long distances constrains the development of integrated supply chain and cost-effective mobilization of biomass, particularly for salvage harvest (
Hughes et al. 2014;
Mansuy et al. 2018). Recent analyses have demonstrated that increases in wildfire have already begun to reduce the availability of wood to meet society's needs for wood products (
Bousfield et al. 2023), and at the same time there are calls for increasing areas set aside for conservation to mitigate climate change (
Mo et al. 2023). Future assessments will need to consider questions such as the amount by which human society will need to reduce wood demand to meet forest conservation targets, and the consequences to GHG emissions if society instead uses more carbon-intensive alternatives. The federal, provincial, and territorial governments each have a variety of policy goals related to forests in the context of their own jurisdictional responsibilities to guide and enforce sustainable forest management practices and standards on public and private lands across the country. The progress towards quantifying the climate change mitigation options for Canadian forests is reviewed in the next section.
---
